dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 for a cardiac arrest call at a restaurant in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Sarah, an EMT with the rescue squad. I see the ambulance is already on scene. What occurred before we arrived?
She was eating dinner when she suddenly grabbed her chest, said it felt really tight, and became unresponsive.
Let's check her responsiveness. Can you tell me what your name is? Can you squeeze my finger?
No response. Eyes are closed. Unresponsive. GCS is 3.
Airway was obstructed initially by food particles and the tongue. OPA is already in place.
Oropharyngeal airway is in the patient's mouth and secure.
Agonal respirations are present at approximately 6 per minute with accessory muscles used. Breath sounds in both lungs are equal.

dialogue.micro_intent: resp - assist ventilation - bvm via mask
dialogue.utterance: We're still using BVM for assisted ventilations with 15 liters per minute of oxygen to the mask.
Carotid pulse is absent. Skin is pale, dry, and cool. What vitals do we have on record?
Blood pressure is 0/0 mmHg.
Respirations are 6 per minute.
Pulse is absent at point of check.
SpO₂ is 0%.
EKG shows no reading - likely flatline or asystole.
AED pads are on, and no shocks indicated. Manual CPR in progress.
Taking over compressions. Depth is at least 2 inches at 100-120 per minute.
Blood glucose was attempted, but we're getting 0. Could mean too little sample or meter issue.
Tell me more about what you observed before she collapsed.
She suddenly complained of her chest feeling tight and dizziness. Then she just slumped forward and didn't respond.
Does she have any other medical conditions or medications?
She has mild asthma and a history of bronchitis. No known allergies.
Did she have anything to eat or drink before this happened?
She was eating a meal. No idea what she had to drink.
Respirations improved slightly to 17 per minute.
Pulse is now detected at 167 beats per minute with the AutoPulse.
Blood pressure is 51/17 mmHg.
SpO₂ is 71%.
EKG now shows sinus tachycardia at roughly 167 bpm.
Epinephrine 1mg has been administered intraosseously.
Intraosseous access is secure at the right humeral head.
ETco2 is now 31. Airway placement is confirmed with capnography and chest rise.
A saline lock has been established via IO line for fluid administration.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia again. Last read showed 135 bpm.
Respirations now at 19 per minute and with better chest rise.
Heart rate is 135; did not drop after second epinephrine.
Blood pressure is up to 138/97 now.
Oxygen saturation is 73%.
Previous rhythm was PEA. Rhythm now indicates ROSC with controlled mechanical compressions.
Let's slightly reposition the King airway to improve breath sounds bilaterally.
No signs of jaw clenching or neck crepitus.
Skin is still pale, though slightly warmer than before ROSC.
Breath sounds in both lungs are improved and equal now.
EKG is still showing sinus tachycardia.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: The airway was suctioned a few times, but no blockage observed. Some residue noted in the tube.
Airway was suctioned at approximately 18:57 using 12Fr soft tip suction.
4-lead EKG is on, and display appears stable.
12-lead ECG just completed and shows abnormal findings including abn Q in V1 and a minimal initial anterior vector.
Following protocol 2-7 for cardiac arrest for ROSC and transport.
Are we transporting to [COMPANY_NAME]?
Patient is secured on the stretcher with a five-point seatbelt restraint and reeves device.
[COMPANY_NAME] is prepped and ready. Due to her condition, we're charging for an emergency transport now.
She has a history of mild asthma and no allergies. She reportedly recently traveled to Egypt. Any travel-related symptoms?
No, nothing like that.
Earlier glucose was 124; we don't have current reading but no access issues.
Epinephrine has been given twice. We might need a second drug box for potential third dose.
Firefighters are on board assisting. En route. EKG is stable.
[COMPANY_NAME], we need a hot hospital bed on arrival. Possible non-STEMI observed.
Patient had a cardiac arrest with ROSC, but rhythm is sinus tach. No STEMI detected.
Pulse remains at 135 with AutoPulse.
Blood pressure is stable at 138/97.
En route EKG shows sinus tach. Findings from 12-lead are flagging for review.
Continuing with transport to [COMPANY_NAME] under ROSC cardiac arrest for evaluation and definitive care.
Autopulse is still running. Monitors are clear.
The King airway was slightly pulled back and is now providing equal breath sounds again.
Breathing is approximately 17 breaths per minute and does not appear labored.
Saturation is at 71% - continue supplemental oxygen.
She was not on any current medications, though. No prior medications or prescriptions observed.
She just had a meal and experienced sudden chest pain but no heartburn symptoms.
Rhythm remains sinus tach with 135 bpm. No new ST issues detected.
EKG showed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and possible aberrant conduction or ventricular premature complexes.
Respiratory failure is also a concern based on initial O2 saturation of 0%. Tune in for any changes.
Skin is still pale but now appears closer to normal temperature with small signs of perfusion return.
SpO₂ recovered slightly to 73%.
Moving toward completion. All lines are secure and patient condition is relatively stable for transport.
Preparing to close doors and go.
Now slightly more pink, could indicate early return of perfusion, but ROSC was some time ago.
No STEMI signs. 12-lead and current EKG unchanged.
Blood pressure now manually measured at 138/97 following ROSC.
Must suction the airway again. Food residue is visible in tube.
Glucose at 124 mg/dL about an hour ago but not rechecked since, no access issues.
En route. All vitals constantly monitored. Prep for receiving hospital.
[COMPANY_NAME] has been alerted. Hot bed and care team ready.
Two EMTs, two firefighters. All protocols clear for transfer of care.
[NAME], do we need any other interventions or reassessments before departure?
No. Vital signs stable. Airway secure. Transport moving under ROSC protocol.
Copy. All set, closing up.
Ambulance heading out. Five-point restraints locked. All additional safety taken.
Awaiting [COMPANY_NAME] arrival for handoff. Stable condition continuing.
Conditions stable since ROSC. No acute changes seen.
Prepare to hand over to [COMPANY_NAME] at --Address Redacted--.
[COMPANY_NAME] line is open. Confirming arrival in five minutes.
All patient care documents available, including treatment and ECG reports.
Conditions and vitals consistent. No changes since in-transit start.
All set for handover. Blood work and vitals on record. No updates needed.
King airway reassessed, no issues. No spontaneous breathing observed.
Oxygen supply currently at 70% saturation. No new issues identified.
Pulse remains at 135 with mechanical support.
Blood pressure steady at 138/97 during transport.
En route. [COMPANY_NAME] expects cardiac arrest with ROSC. 12-lead displayed for review.
No significant deviations in vitals observed. Routine checks clear.
Recheck breath sounds after airway adjustment - still equal on both sides.
Skin remains stable: no signs of mottling or cyanosis since ROSC.
GCS is still 3 with very minimal response.
[COMPANY_NAME] on alert, as per. No further detours needed.
SpO₂ has dropped slightly to 68% despite ongoing supplementation, keep oxygen flow high.
No acute changes in cardiac function reported since 1908 hours, latest pulse check.
Status stable. Continue under same transport code.
EKG remains sinus tach with no sign of acute injury.
King tube secure. No tube displacement confirmed.
Blood pressure manually verified at 136/95 - consistent with AutoPulse assisted.
IO line clear, no signs of clotting or dislodgement.
EKG and capnography displaying stable rhythm and adequate airway patency.
No epinephrine needed since last dose at 19:02.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: No additional clearing required. Airway remains unobstructed.
Autopulse and BVM assisting with ventilation and compressions consistently.
Manual bag assisting now as requested.
Plasma and crystalloid bolus scheduled in on arrival, as needed.
Secondary assessment will be completed on site at [COMPANY_NAME] for full imaging and lab work.
Vitals and notes have been passed to receiving nurse and ED.
